row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business data and my customers' data for analytics and marketing. Does this comply with India's data protection laws, and what limits — if any — does RBI or DPDP Act place on how far this license actually extends?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant handling personal data of thousands of end-customers
row.answer:
This is a genuinely unsettled area, and the ToS itself does not resolve it cleanly.

**What the ToS does say:** Part A §1.2 grants Razorpay a broad, royalty-free, irrevocable, transferable, and sub-licensable license to use 'Your data, Your customer's data, information, content, trademarks, logos and any other materials/information You upload or make available.' The clause permits use for operating and improving the platform, for Razorpay's own marketing and promotional materials (without requiring further consent from you), and for analytics — with Razorpay retaining ownership of aggregated and anonymised results. The clause does add the qualifier 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.'

**What the ToS does not say:** The clause does not define what 'Applicable Laws' means in this context, nor does it enumerate which specific regulatory frameworks govern the scope of this license. Critically, it does not:
- Specify how the license interacts with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act), which imposes consent and purpose-limitation obligations on 'Data Fiduciaries' — a role you may occupy with respect to your customers' data;
- Clarify whether your customers' personal data (as distinct from your own business data) can lawfully be sub-licensed to third-party service providers under RBI's guidelines on outsourcing and data localisation (e.g., RBI's 2021 Master Direction on Payment Aggregators and Payment Gateways);
- Define what 'aggregated and anonymised' means operationally, or whether the anonymisation standard meets any regulatory threshold;
- Address whether your grant of this license on behalf of your customers is legally sufficient, or whether Razorpay must independently obtain consent from end-customers under the DPDP Act;
- State whether the 'irrevocable' nature of the license survives account termination and, if so, for how long — which may conflict with data-erasure rights under emerging Indian data protection law.

**Why this matters for you:** As the merchant, you may be a Data Fiduciary under the DPDP Act with independent obligations to your customers. Granting a broad downstream license without understanding its regulatory limits could expose you to regulatory liability that the indemnity clause in §1.2 would not protect you from — in fact, that indemnity runs *in Razorpay's favour*, not yours.

**Recommended escalation paths:**
1. **Razorpay Support / Legal Team** — Request a written clarification on how §1.2 is scoped against DPDP Act obligations and RBI PA/PG guidelines, and whether Razorpay acts as a Data Processor or co-Fiduciary with respect to customer data.
2. **Independent Legal Counsel** — Engage a privacy or fintech lawyer to assess whether your acceptance of §1.2 is compatible with your own DPDP Act obligations before going live.
3. **RBI / MeitY Guidance** — Monitor RBI's evolving circulars on Payment Aggregators and MeitY's rules under the DPDP Act (rules not yet fully notified as of mid-2025) for authoritative boundary-setting on data use by payment intermediaries.

No confident resolution can be given here — the interaction between this contractual license and India's layered regulatory framework remains genuinely unclear.

row.question: Razorpay's ToS says it can use my business data and customer data for analytics and retain ownership of derived reports. row.answer:
This is a genuinely uncertain area where the ToS provides a partial answer but leaves critical boundaries undefined.

**What the ToS does say (Part A §1.2):** Razorpay is granted a broad, royalty-free, irrevocable, transferable, and sub-licensable licence over your data and your customers' data. It may use those materials for analytics, retain ownership of derived aggregated and anonymised reports, share them with 'Group Entities and third-party service providers', and use them in marketing — all without requiring incremental consent from you. The clause does add a limiting phrase: 'in accordance with Applicable Laws.'

**What the ToS does not say:** The clause does not define what 'Applicable Laws' means in this context, nor does it specify which laws govern cross-border transfers of derived data. It does not:
- Identify whether RBI's Payment Aggregator / Payment Gateway (PA/PG) Guidelines (March 2020 and subsequent circulars) restrict how payment-related customer data may be shared with overseas entities or sub-licensees;
- Clarify whether the data-localisation requirements under RBI's Storage of Payment System Data directions (April 2018) apply to derived/aggregated reports, or only to raw transaction data;
- Address how the forthcoming Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA) obligations interact with this broad licence — particularly around the lawful basis for processing customer personal data and any cross-border transfer restrictions;
- Define 'anonymised' to a legal standard (e.g., whether it meets the irreversibility threshold that regulators would require before data falls outside personal-data protection rules);
- Specify whether the sub-licences granted to overseas Group Entities or third-party service providers are subject to any contractual data-processing safeguards, standard contractual clauses, or RBI approval.

**Why this creates genuine ambiguity:** The clause explicitly defers to 'Applicable Laws' without defining their scope or hierarchy. RBI guidelines on payment data localisation, NPCI operational circulars, and the DPDPA each impose distinct obligations that may constrain — or even conflict with — the broad licence Razorpay has taken. Whether a derived, aggregated report constitutes 'payment system data' under RBI rules, or 'personal data' under the DPDPA, is not answered here and may depend on the specific data points included.

**Recommended escalation path:**
1. **Razorpay Support / Data Protection Officer:** Request Razorpay's written clarification on how it interprets 'Applicable Laws' in §1.2, which specific RBI/NPCI circulars it considers binding on derived-data sharing, and whether a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) is available.
2. **Independent Legal Counsel:** Engage a lawyer specialising in Indian fintech and data-privacy law to assess your exposure under the DPDPA, RBI PA/PG Guidelines, and RBI's data-localisation circular before onboarding sensitive customer data.
3. **RBI / NPCI Guidance:** For authoritative interpretation of whether aggregated/anonymised payment-derived reports are subject to localisation or transfer restrictions, consider seeking clarification directly from RBI's Department of Payment and Settlement Systems or reviewing NPCI's published operational guidelines.

In short, the clause establishes a very wide licence in Razorpay's favour, but the regulatory boundaries of that licence — particularly for cross-border data flows and sub-licensing — remain undefined in the ToS itself and will depend on external regulatory interpretation.

row.question: If I build a white-label checkout interface using Razorpay's APIs and brand it with my own logo, does the restriction on 'derivative works' and 'marks' in the ToS conflict with RBI's co-branding guidelines for payment aggregators, and which set of rules takes precedence?
row.persona: A fintech startup founder who has received RBI's payment aggregator authorisation and wants to offer a co-branded checkout experience to their merchant clients, using Razorpay as the underlying PA infrastructure.
row.answer:
This is a genuinely unresolved question and no confident answer can be offered here.

What the Razorpay ToS does say: Part A §1.1 grants you only a 'personal, revocable, non-exclusive, non-transferable, limited right to access the Platform and make use of the Platform and the Services.' The same clause explicitly prohibits you from creating a 'derivative work,' using Razorpay's marks without explicit consent, and sub-licensing or transferring any right in the Services. It also requires you not to remove or obscure any proprietary rights notices.

What the ToS does not say: The clause does not define where the boundary lies between a permissible custom integration and a prohibited 'derivative work.' It does not address how its IP restrictions interact with RBI's Master Directions on Payment Aggregators and Payment Gateways (or any co-branding circulars issued by RBI/NPCI), which may independently govern how a licensed PA may present its brand alongside an underlying infrastructure provider. The ToS contains no carve-out or safe harbour for regulatory co-branding obligations, nor does it specify which instrument — the ToS or applicable RBI/NPCI directions — controls in the event of a conflict. The phrase 'explicit consent' is used without defining the form, scope, or duration of such consent.

Because the ToS defers implicitly to external regulation without defining the boundary, a genuine regulatory gap exists. Depending on how RBI's PA Master Directions are interpreted, your co-branding arrangement might be required, permitted, or restricted — and the ToS offers no guidance on reconciling those obligations with its IP restrictions.

Recommended escalation steps:
1. Contact Razorpay's merchant support and legal/partnerships team directly to request written clarification on what co-branding or white-label use cases are covered under 'explicit consent' and whether a separate IP licence or addendum is available.
2. Engage independent legal counsel with payments-regulation expertise to analyse whether your proposed checkout interface constitutes a 'derivative work' under Indian IP law and how RBI's PA Master Directions interact with the ToS restrictions.
3. Seek guidance from RBI's Department of Payment and Settlement Systems and review NPCI circulars on co-branding to understand any mandatory disclosure or branding obligations that may apply to your licence.

Do not proceed with the co-branded deployment until written clarity is obtained from Razorpay and a legal review is complete.

clause_citations.verbatim_excerpt: Razorpay PA grants You a personal, revocable, non-exclusive, non-transferable, limited right to access the Platform and make use of the Platform and the Services in accordance with the Terms. You shall not download, copy, create a derivative work, modify, reverse engineer, reverse assemble, transmit or otherwise attempt to discover any source code, sell, assign, sub-license, grant a security interest in or otherwise transfer any right in the Services or marks. You do not have the right to use any of our marks without explicit consent from us.

ambiguity.what_is_known: Part A §1.1 prohibits creating derivative works, using Razorpay's marks without explicit consent, and sub-licensing or transferring any rights in the Services. It grants only a limited, personal, non-transferable licence to access the Platform.
ambiguity.what_is_missing: The ToS does not define the boundary between a permissible custom integration and a prohibited derivative work, does not address how its IP restrictions interact with RBI's PA Master Directions or NPCI co-branding rules, provides no conflict-resolution mechanism specifying which instrument prevails when regulatory obligations and ToS restrictions diverge, and does not define the form or scope of 'explicit consent' for mark usage.

retrieval_trace.silence_hint: The ToS defers to external regulation (RBI / NPCI / state law) without defining boundaries.

row.question: We're building a feature that lets enterprise clients pay invoices directly through our Razorpay Payment Aggregator integration. One of our clients wants to pay a single invoice worth INR 30,00,000 in one transaction. Will Razorpay PA be able to process that?
row.persona: backend_engineer
row.answer: No, Razorpay PA cannot process that transaction. The maximum value of a single transaction that Razorpay PA can process is INR 25,00,000 (Twenty-Five Lakhs). A payment of INR 30,00,000 exceeds this limit and would need to be split into multiple transactions each within the INR 25,00,000 cap, or handled through a different payment arrangement.

answer_branches.answer: If you pay via Fee Credits, the liability cap under Part A §7.2 is capped at one month's worth of fees paid — in your case, this would be calculated based on the fees deducted from your Fee Credit balance over the preceding month for the specific Service giving rise to the liability. For example, if INR 100 in Fee Credits were consumed in fees over that month, your maximum recoverable amount from Razorpay PA would be INR 100. Note also that any loss contributed to by you or your third-party providers will proportionately reduce Razorpay PA's liability.

row.question: If a fraudulent transaction on my Razorpay integration results in a chargeback, exactly which rules and timelines apply — and does RBI's chargeback framework or NPCI's dispute resolution mechanism take precedence over what Razorpay's Terms say?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant experiencing a disputed fraudulent transaction
row.answer:
This is a genuinely ambiguous area where Razorpay's Terms of Service provide only a partial answer, and the full picture requires looking beyond the document itself.

**What the ToS does say:** Part B Part I §4.3 states that 'if the Fraudulent Transaction results in a Chargeback, then such Chargeback shall be resolved in accordance with the provisions set out in the Terms.' This confirms that a chargeback pathway exists for fraudulent transactions and that the Terms govern it — but only to the extent that the Terms themselves define the process.

**What the ToS does not say:** The clause does not specify: (a) which external regulatory framework — RBI's chargeback circulars, NPCI's dispute resolution rules for UPI/RuPay, card network rules (Visa/Mastercard), or applicable state/central law — controls in the event of a conflict or gap; (b) the precise timelines a merchant must follow to raise or respond to a chargeback; (c) how liability is allocated between Razorpay, the acquiring bank, and the merchant when fraud is alleged but not yet proven; and (d) whether RBI's Zero Liability / Limited Liability framework for unauthorised transactions overrides any contractual term in the ToS.

The clause effectively defers resolution to 'the Terms,' but the Terms themselves appear to defer — at least implicitly — to external regulation without drawing clear boundaries between what the contract controls and what RBI/NPCI mandates control. This creates a regulatory gap that the document alone cannot resolve.

**Recommended escalation path:**
1. **Razorpay Merchant Support** — Request a written clarification of the specific chargeback procedure, timelines, and liability allocation applicable to your payment method (card, UPI, netbanking).
2. **Legal Counsel** — Engage a payments-law specialist to assess how RBI's Master Direction on Prepaid Payment Instruments, the RBI circular on customer protection in unauthorised electronic transactions (DBR.No.Leg.BC.78/09.07.005/2017-18), and relevant NPCI operating guidelines interact with your merchant agreement.
3. **RBI / NPCI Guidance** — If the dispute involves a systemic or regulatory question, the RBI Ombudsman for Digital Transactions or NPCI's dispute portal may provide authoritative direction.

No confident resolution can be offered here without knowing your specific payment instrument, the applicable card/network scheme rules, and how Razorpay's internal chargeback policy operationalises §4.3.

clause_citations.verbatim_excerpt: Subject to clause 4.2 above, if the Fraudulent Transaction results in a Chargeback, then such Chargeback shall be resolved in accordance with the provisions set out in the Terms.

ambiguity.what_is_known: Part B Part I §4.3 confirms that fraudulent transactions leading to chargebacks are governed by Razorpay's Terms, establishing a contractual framework for resolution.
ambiguity.what_is_missing: The ToS does not define which external regulatory body's rules (RBI circulars, NPCI operating procedures, card network scheme rules) take precedence in the event of a conflict, nor does it specify chargeback timelines, liability allocation between parties, or how RBI's unauthorised transaction liability framework interacts with the contractual chargeback process.

row.question: If a fraudulent transaction is detected after settlement funds have already been credited to my account, how exactly will the dispute be resolved — and what specific steps or timelines apply to me as the merchant?
row.persona: E-commerce merchant who has received settlement for a transaction later flagged as fraudulent
row.answer:
This is a genuinely uncertain area where the Razorpay Terms of Service provide only partial guidance, and a confident resolution cannot be offered.

**What the ToS does say:** Part B Part I §4.2 explicitly acknowledges this scenario — where a fraudulent transaction has already been settled to you — and states that any resulting dispute 'shall be resolved in accordance with RBI's notification DBR.No.Leg.BC.78/09.07.005/2017-18 dated July 6, 2017, read with RBI's notification DBOD.LEG.BC 86/09.07.007/2001-02 dated April 8, 2002, and other notifications, circulars and guidelines issued by the RBI in this regard from time to time.' This confirms that a post-settlement fraud dispute mechanism exists and is governed by RBI frameworks.

**What the ToS does NOT say:** The clause stops at the deferral itself and leaves several critical operational questions unanswered:
- It does not specify what procedural steps Razorpay or the merchant must follow once a post-settlement fraud flag is raised.
- It does not define timelines — for notification to the merchant, for fund recovery attempts, or for the merchant's right to respond or contest.
- It does not clarify whether Razorpay will initiate a chargeback-style clawback of already-settled funds, or what the merchant's liability exposure is if the RBI circulars assign liability to the acquirer vs. the merchant.
- It does not explain how the phrase 'other notifications, circulars and guidelines issued by the RBI from time to time' is tracked or communicated to merchants — meaning the applicable regulatory framework could shift without explicit ToS amendment.
- It does not address scenarios involving NPCI rules (relevant for UPI-based transactions) or card network chargeback rules that may run in parallel.

**Why this matters:** The two RBI circulars cited (the 2017 customer protection circular and the 2002 circular on fraud reporting) establish frameworks primarily oriented toward customer liability limits and bank reporting obligations. How those frameworks translate into specific merchant-facing obligations through an aggregator like Razorpay is not self-evident from the clause.

**Recommended escalation paths:**
1. **Razorpay Merchant Support / Account Manager** — Request a written explanation of the exact post-settlement fraud dispute procedure, including timelines, fund recovery process, and how merchants are notified.
2. **Legal Counsel** — Engage a payments-law specialist to review the two cited RBI circulars and assess your actual liability exposure as a merchant under the aggregator model.
3. **RBI / NPCI Guidance** — If the dispute involves a specific payment instrument (e.g., UPI, card), consider seeking clarification from the relevant regulator or reviewing publicly available FAQs and master circulars directly.

row.question: If a new tax levy or a mid-year GST rate revision increases my effective tax burden on Razorpay's fees, is there a cap or ceiling on how much additional tax cost I can be required to absorb, and will Razorpay notify me in advance before passing through the revised charge?
row.persona: Mid-sized e-commerce merchant reviewing cost predictability and budgeting obligations under the Razorpay payment aggregator agreement
row.answer:
Based on Part A §3.2 of the Razorpay Terms of Service, it is established that fees quoted to you are exclusive of applicable taxes, and — critically — that 'any statutory variations in applicable taxes during the subsistence of these Terms shall be borne by You.' This means the contractual intent is clear that tax increases flowing from legislative or regulatory change are passed through to the merchant rather than absorbed by Razorpay.

However, several important questions remain genuinely unresolved by the ToS text:

1. **No cap or ceiling is defined.** The clause does not specify any upper threshold, percentage limit, or 'reasonableness' ceiling on the quantum of statutory tax variation a merchant must absorb. Whether an extraordinary or compounded tax increase could ever be contested is not addressed.

2. **No advance-notice obligation is specified.** The ToS does not state whether Razorpay must provide prior written notice before applying a revised tax charge, how much lead time (if any) would be given, or through what channel the notification would occur. The phrase 'from time to time' implies Razorpay may update tax charges as circumstances change, but the mechanics and timing are left open.

3. **'Applicable taxes' is not exhaustively defined.** It is unclear whether this covers only GST, or could extend to future levies such as digital services taxes, transaction-level surcharges, or state-level imposts that may emerge.

4. **No dispute or objection mechanism is outlined.** The clause does not describe any process by which a merchant could challenge a tax pass-through it believes is incorrectly calculated or improperly characterised as a 'statutory variation.'

Given these gaps, it would not be appropriate to assume either that there is a protective cap or that none could ever be negotiated. The situation may also be influenced by RBI's Payment Aggregator guidelines and GST rules under the CGST Act, which operate independently of the Razorpay ToS.

**Recommended escalation steps:**
- **Razorpay Merchant Support / Account Manager:** Request explicit clarification on the advance-notice process and whether any contractual amendments can specify notification timelines.
- **Legal Counsel:** Have a solicitor review whether the open-ended pass-through obligation is enforceable without a reasonableness or notice qualifier under Indian contract law, and whether any implied duty of good faith applies.
- **RBI / NPCI Guidance:** Consult the RBI's Payment Aggregator and Payment Gateway guidelines and applicable GST circulars to understand the regulatory floor for such disclosures.

In summary, while the obligation to bear statutory tax variations is stated, the absence of caps, notice requirements, and dispute mechanisms creates material ambiguity that warrants direct engagement with Razorpay and independent legal advice before relying on any assumed protections.

clause_citations.verbatim_excerpt: Fees are exclusive of applicable taxes and Razorpay PA will charge such applicable taxes on the fees from time to time. You agree that any statutory variations in applicable taxes during the subsistence of these Terms shall be borne by You.

ambiguity.what_is_known: Part A §3.2 confirms that all quoted fees exclude taxes, that Razorpay may charge applicable taxes 'from time to time,' and that the merchant contractually bears any statutory variation in those taxes for the duration of the agreement.
ambiguity.what_is_missing: The ToS does not define any cap or ceiling on the magnitude of tax variation the merchant must absorb, does not specify any advance-notice obligation or notification timeline before revised charges are applied, does not enumerate which taxes qualify as 'applicable taxes,' and provides no merchant dispute or objection mechanism for challenging a tax pass-through. The phrase 'from time to time' implies unilateral flexibility for Razorpay without specifying thresholds, triggers, or procedural safeguards.

retrieval_trace.silence_hint: The ToS uses qualitative language ('reasonable', 'as determined') without specifying thresholds.

row.question: If I build a white-label checkout experience on top of Razorpay's platform and co-brand it with my own logo, does Razorpay's ToS or any external regulation (such as RBI payment aggregator guidelines) govern who actually owns the resulting user interface and branding assets?
row.persona: A fintech startup founder who has developed a heavily customised checkout flow using Razorpay's APIs and wants to know whether co-branding or derivative UI work could conflict with Razorpay's IP ownership clause or RBI/NPCI regulations on payment aggregator branding.
row.answer:
This question sits at the intersection of Razorpay's intellectual property clause and external regulatory requirements, and the ToS does not resolve it cleanly.

What the ToS does say: Part A §1.1 establishes that Razorpay (and its successors/licensors) retains 'sole ownership of all rights, title and interest in the Services, including the Platform, and all underlying intellectual property rights.' It also explicitly prohibits merchants from creating 'a derivative work' or using Razorpay's marks without explicit consent. The licence granted is described as 'personal, revocable, non-exclusive, non-transferable, limited' — meaning you are permitted to use the platform but are not granted any ownership stake in it.

What the ToS does not say: The clause does not define where Razorpay's platform ends and a merchant's independently developed UI begins. It does not address whether a merchant-originated user interface layer built on top of the APIs constitutes a 'derivative work' of the platform. Crucially, it does not describe how RBI's Payment Aggregator (PA) guidelines or NPCI's branding/co-branding rules interact with or override these IP restrictions. The ToS merely defers to 'applicable law' in general terms without mapping those external rules onto specific IP scenarios.

Why this matters: RBI's 2020 PA/PG guidelines and subsequent circulars impose specific disclosure, branding, and operational requirements on payment aggregators and their merchants. It is not clear from the ToS alone whether compliance with those external rules would be treated by Razorpay as permitted use or as a violation of the derivative-work prohibition.

Given this genuine gap, it would be inadvisable to proceed with a co-branded or white-label deployment without first: (1) contacting Razorpay support or your dedicated account manager to request explicit written consent under §1.1; (2) consulting qualified legal counsel with expertise in Indian fintech and IP law to assess whether your UI qualifies as a derivative work; and (3) reviewing current RBI Payment Aggregator guidelines and any applicable NPCI circulars to understand mandatory branding obligations that may affect how you may or may not use the Razorpay platform visually.

clause_citations.verbatim_excerpt: We (and our successors and licensors, as applicable) remain the sole owner of all rights, title and interest in the Services, including the Platform, and all underlying intellectual property rights (whether registered or not). Razorpay PA grants You a personal, revocable, non-exclusive, non-transferable, limited right to access the Platform and make use of the Platform and the Services in accordance with the Terms.

You shall not download, copy, create a derivative work, modify, reverse engineer, reverse assemble, transmit or otherwise attempt to discover any source code, sell, assign, sub-license, grant a security interest in or otherwise transfer any right in the Services or marks.
You do not have the right to use any of our marks without explicit consent from us.
